title: Civilian Protection in Islamic Law and the Biblical Tradition
subtitle: Who May Be Fought, and Who May Never Be Targeted, Across Two Textual Traditions
summary: Islamic jurisprudence developed an explicit doctrine of non-combatant immunity grounded in Quranic verses, the Prophet Muhammad's recorded practice, and a genuine cross-school juristic consensus — a doctrine this article examines honestly alongside the difficulty posed by Quran 9:5. The Hebrew Bible's herem texts, by contrast, describe the total destruction of conquered populations including women and children, presented here at full strength alongside the mainstream Jewish and Christian responses to them. A closing section considers, with careful attribution, how both traditions' ancient war texts have surfaced in modern conflicts — a question about adherents' conduct that is distinct from what the founding texts themselves establish.

seo.metaTitle: Civilian Protection in Islamic Law and the Biblical Tradition
seo.metaDescription: How Islamic jurisprudence and the biblical tradition each address who may be fought and who must be spared — hadith, the sword verse, herem texts, and modern invocations, compared fairly.

sections.title: Introduction: Who May Be Fought, and Who May Never Be Targeted
sections.body:
Every tradition that permits warfare at all must answer two distinct questions: who may licitly be fought, and who may never be targeted regardless of the cause. Islamic jurisprudence and the Hebrew and Christian scriptures both address this question, but through very different textual histories. Islamic law developed an explicit, detailed doctrine of non-combatant immunity grounded in Quranic verses, in the Prophet Muhammad's recorded practice, and in centuries of juristic elaboration across the four Sunni schools of law. The Bible, by contrast, contains texts that establish comparable protections and also a small number of texts — the herem, or "ban," passages describing the conquest of Canaan — that appear to command the opposite: the total destruction of entire populations, explicitly including women and children.

This article examines both traditions at full strength. On the Islamic side, it presents the Quranic and hadith basis for civilian protection, the near-unanimous juristic consensus that followed, and the honest difficulty posed by Quran 9:5, the so-called "sword verse," including both the mainstream contextual reading and a genuine classical abrogation-based reading. On the biblical side, it presents the herem texts at full strength, alongside the mainstream Jewish and Christian interpretive responses to them and the distinct question of the New Testament's ethic of enemy-love. A closing section considers, with careful attribution, how both traditions' ancient war texts have surfaced in modern conflicts and modern warfare more broadly — a question about adherents' and states' conduct that is separate from, and does not resolve, what the founding texts themselves establish.

sections.title: The Qur'an on the Limits of Fighting
sections.body:
Quran 2:190 states: "Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you but do not transgress. Indeed, Allah does not like transgressors." Addressed to the early Muslim community facing armed conflict with Mecca, the verse frames permission to fight in narrowly conditional terms: only against those actively fighting, and only within a limit — "do not transgress" (la ta'tadu) — that classical and modern exegetes alike read as encompassing restrictions on methods and targets, later elaborated in detail by the jurists discussed below. The verse does not itself list protected categories, but its structure — permission bounded by an explicit non-transgression clause — is the textual seed from which Muslim jurists developed the more detailed rules of engagement examined in this article.

Quran 5:32 states: "Because of that, We decreed upon the Children of Israel that whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land - it is as if he had slain mankind entirely. And whoever saves one - it is as if he had saved mankind entirely. And our messengers had certainly come to them with clear proofs. Then indeed many of them, [even] after that, throughout the land, were transgressors." This verse's immediate subject is individual homicide law as given to the Children of Israel, with a stated exception for lawful capital punishment or the suppression of "corruption in the land" — it is not, in its primary sense, a rule of war. Muslim jurists and preachers have nonetheless invoked it consistently, across the classical and modern periods, as underwriting a general Quranic principle: that the sanctity of a single human life carries the moral weight of humanity as a whole, and that killing outside strictly bounded exceptions is a graver matter than an isolated act. That principle, applied to the conduct of war, is one of the sources jurists cite alongside 2:190 and the hadith material below when explaining why non-combatants may not be killed as a matter of course.

sections.title: The Prophet's Practice and Abu Bakr's Instructions to the Army
sections.body:
The clearest evidence for a specific prohibition on killing women and children comes from the Prophet Muhammad's own recorded practice rather than from Quranic text alone. Ibn Umar reported that during one of the Prophet's military expeditions, a woman was found among the slain; when this was reported to the Prophet, he denounced it and forbade the killing of women and children in war. This report is recorded, in closely similar wording, in both Sahih al-Bukhari (hadith 3014) and Sahih Muslim (hadith 1744), a status hadith scholars term muttafaqun 'alayhi — agreed upon by the two most rigorously vetted hadith collections in Sunni Islam. Related reports on the same prohibition are attributed to additional narrators, including Buraydah, al-Aswad ibn Sari', Ibn 'Abbas, and al-Sa'b ibn Jaththama, giving the rule an unusually broad base of transmission for a single point of law.

This categorical rule sits alongside a separate, frequently discussed report attributed to that same companion, al-Sa'b ibn Jaththama, recorded in Sahih Muslim (hadith 1745) under the chapter heading "Permissibility of killing women and children in night raids, so long as it is not done deliberately." Asked about women and children of polytheists killed during night raids, the Prophet is reported to have replied, "They are of them" (hum minhum). Classical commentators treated this as a narrow qualification rather than a reversal of the general rule: al-Nawawi, in his commentary on Sahih Muslim, and Ibn Hajar al-'Asqalani, in Fath al-Bari, both explain the remark as addressing incidental deaths occurring in the dark, when combatants and non-combatants could not be visually distinguished from one another, rather than as license to target women and children knowingly; where such a distinction could be made, jurists held, the categorical prohibition continued to govern. The report is one classical jurists had to reconcile with the general rule, via this distinction between incidental and deliberate killing, rather than one that simply reinforces the prohibition without complication, and it continues to be cited in modern discussions of incidental civilian harm in warfare.

The principle was carried into institutional practice within roughly a year of the Prophet's death. According to Imam Malik ibn Anas's Muwatta (Book of Jihad) and al-Tabari's History, when Caliph Abu Bakr al-Siddiq dispatched the Muslim armies toward Syria, he walked alongside Yazid ibn Abi Sufyan, one of the appointed commanders, and gave him a set of instructions before the army marched. He is reported to have said, in substance: do not kill a woman, a child, or an aged and infirm person; do not cut down fruit-bearing trees; do not destroy an inhabited dwelling; do not slaughter a sheep or a camel except for food; do not burn or scatter bees; do not steal from the spoils of war; and do not act with cowardice. He is further reported to have told Yazid that the army would encounter people who had devoted their lives entirely to worship in monasteries — monks — and that these were to be left undisturbed, along with what they had devoted themselves to.

The same reported instruction, in the same breath, continues with a considerably less comfortable clause that this article should not pass over: Abu Bakr is also recorded telling Yazid that the army would find people who had shaved the middle of their heads, leaving a ring or band of hair around it, and that the army was to strike what they had shaved with the sword. This second group is not the monastics protected in the preceding clause. Commentators on the passage generally identify the described hairstyle with a distinctive tonsure worn by certain Byzantine-affiliated clergy and monastic orders in Roman Syria, and treat it as marking a group distinct from the ascetic, world-renouncing monks addressed immediately before — but the primary sources do not themselves explain the basis for the distinction, and this article is not in a position to resolve, from the text alone, why one tonsured religious group was to be left alone and another struck. What can be said plainly is this: the single passage most often cited as Abu Bakr's founding charge for protecting monastics also authorizes killing a second group identified by a religious marker, in the very next clause, and a fair presentation of this precedent has to include that rather than quoting only its more comfortable half.

Later jurists treated this episode as an authoritative early precedent, alongside the Quranic and hadith material above, for extending protection to categories — the elderly and monastics explicitly, and, in the juristic elaboration that followed, hired laborers and other non-combatants — not individually itemized in the Quran's own war verses. The same episode, read in full rather than selectively, is a reminder that even this centerpiece precedent is not a clean, uncomplicated protection list.

sections.title: Juristic Consensus Across the Four Schools — and Its Limits
sections.body:
By the classical period, jurists across the Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali schools had converted this Quranic and hadith foundation into detailed doctrine. The Andalusian jurist Ibn Rushd (Averroes), in his comparative law survey Bidayat al-Mujtahid, records that the schools held no disagreement on two specific points: it is permissible to fight adult male polytheists who are actively waging war, and it is not permitted to kill women or children so long as they are not themselves fighting. This narrow point — the immunity of non-combatant women and children specifically — functioned as a genuine ijma, or scholarly consensus, across the classical schools, and it is the strongest textually and historically attested claim this article makes about Islamic law's protection of civilians.

Ibn Rushd also records that the schools disagreed on the underlying legal rationale for the rule, and that this disagreement had real consequences for less clear-cut categories. Jurists who held that the basis for permitting killing was disbelief itself were, on his account, more willing to extend permissible targeting to non-combatant adult men, including peasants and hired laborers who took no part in the fighting. Jurists who held instead that the basis was the capacity to fight — a position associated particularly with the Hanafi school — extended protection more broadly to anyone lacking that capacity: the elderly, the disabled, monastics, and unarmed peasants and laborers alike. In practice, most later fiqh manuals absorbed the more protective reading into their standard lists of categories exempt from deliberate targeting — women, children, the elderly, the insane, monks and other non-combatant religious figures, and hired laborers — while the underlying scholarly disagreement about why these categories were protected persisted. Contemporary academic treatments of Islamic laws of war, such as the Yaqeen Institute's paper "War, Islam, and the Sanctity of Life: Non-Aggression in the Islamic Code of Combat" and John Kelsay's Arguing the Just War in Islam, generally present the fuller protective list as the settled classical doctrine, even though the point of strict, cross-school ijma recorded by Ibn Rushd is narrower — limited specifically to non-combatant women and children.

sections.title: Quran 9:5 in Its Textual Neighborhood: Two Classical Readings
sections.body:
No honest discussion of civilian protection in Islamic law can omit Quran 9:5, sometimes called ayat al-sayf, the "sword verse." It reads: "And when the sacred months have passed, then kill the polytheists wherever you find them and capture them and besiege them and sit in wait for them at every place of ambush. But if they should repent, establish prayer, and give zakah, let them [go] on their way. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." Read in isolation, this verse states an apparently unconditional command to kill polytheists unless they repent, without explicit restriction to combatants — and it is the single most frequently cited Quranic verse both by critics who argue that the Qur'an licenses indiscriminate violence and, historically, by some classical jurists who argued that it superseded earlier, more restrictive verses.

The verse does not stand alone. It opens a short, tightly connected passage near the beginning of Surah at-Tawba. The verse immediately before it, Quran 9:4, reads: "Excepted are those with whom you made a treaty among the polytheists and then they have not been deficient toward you in anything or supported anyone against you; so complete for them their treaty until their term [has ended]. Indeed, Allah loves the righteous [who fear Him]." This explicitly exempts from the following command any polytheist group that had kept its treaty obligations with the Muslims in full. The verse immediately after, Quran 9:6, reads: "And if any one of the polytheists seeks your protection, then grant him protection so that he may hear the words of Allah. Then deliver him to his place of safety. That is because they are a people who do not know." This instructs that any individual polytheist who sought protection was to be granted it, given the opportunity to hear the Quran, and then delivered safely to a place of security. Verse 9:7, not quoted here at length, reaffirms a similar treaty exception. Read as a textual unit, then, the very passage that contains the "sword verse" also contains, in the same breath, both a treaty-honoring exemption and an individual asylum guarantee.

The mainstream contextual reading, held by the majority of classical and nearly all contemporary exegetes, ties 9:5 to its stated occasion: the surrounding verses of the same sura identify the target as specific Arabian polytheist tribes who had repeatedly broken treaties with the Muslim community and initiated hostilities, not polytheists or non-Muslims as a category. This reading is associated especially with the Hanafi school of law and has been argued at length by modern scholars including the Egyptian Grand Mufti Mahmoud Shaltut and the Syrian jurist Wahbah al-Zuhayli, both well documented as proponents of a contextual, defensive reading of the Quran's war verses generally; 9:4 and 9:6 sitting inside the same short passage as 9:5 is, independent of any single scholar's precise wording, the internal textual evidence contextual readers most often point to for the claim that the chapter did not intend a general command against all non-combatants.

A second, minority-but-genuinely-classical reading held that 9:5 abrogated (naskh) a substantial number of earlier, more conciliatory verses. Ibn Kathir's tafsir records this position as held by the early exegete al-Dahhak ibn Muzahim, who is reported to have said the verse abrogated every peace agreement, treaty, and fixed term the Prophet held with idolaters; the jurist Ibn al-'Arabi went further, holding that 9:5 abrogated some 124 verses counseling patience or forbearance toward unbelievers; and al-Tabari's tafsir presents the verse, drawing on companion-era narrations, as a general command to fight polytheists until conversion. Surveys of the classical abrogation literature record the total number of Quranic verses considered abrogated by some other verse — not solely by the sword verse, but across the whole Qur'an — growing across the medieval period, from roughly 138 in the work of al-Nahhas to as many as 238 in that of Ibn Salama. Al-Nahhas's list is not commonly broken down by which specific verse did the abrogating in each case, but of Ibn Salama's 238 total, roughly half are specifically attributed to the sword verse itself, with the remainder attributed to a range of other abrogating verses. This was a genuine position within classical Islamic legal theory, held by identifiable named scholars, not a modern invention, and it should be presented as such. It was not, however, the only or majority classical position: most classical jurists and virtually all contemporary scholarship hold the contextual reading instead, and 9:4 and 9:6 remain part of the same textual unit under either interpretation.

sections.title: Casualty Estimates Across the Prophet's Military Campaigns
sections.body:
A separate, empirical line of argument for the practical scope of restraint during the Prophet's lifetime comes from historians' attempts to tally total casualties across the roughly eighty military expeditions (ghazawat and saraya) traditionally counted during his prophethood, the great majority of which involved no fighting at all. The historian and jurist Muhammad Hamidullah, in his study Battlefields of the Prophet Muhammad: A Contribution to Muslim Military History, calculated Muslim battle deaths across the entirety of the Prophet's campaigns at approximately 150. Other scholars working from the same classical maghazi (campaign-narrative) sources have produced a comparable combined total: the Indian scholar Maulana Wahiduddin Khan arrived at a combined figure of 259 Muslims and 759 non-Muslims killed (1,018 total), a figure he presents as covering the Prophet's campaigns as a whole, though the underlying combat deaths are concentrated overwhelmingly in a handful of major engagements — chiefly Badr, Uhud, and Hunayn — since the great majority of the roughly eighty expeditions saw no fighting at all. These two figures converge on a widely cited order-of-magnitude estimate of roughly one thousand total combat deaths, on every side, across more than two decades of intermittent warfare touching a rapidly expanding community.

These numbers should be read with the caution appropriate to any casualty estimate drawn from ancient sources. They rest on the maghazi and sira literature compiled by Muslim historians — chiefly Ibn Ishaq's biography of the Prophet as preserved by Ibn Hisham, and al-Waqidi's Kitab al-Maghazi — one to two centuries after the events they describe, using methods of transmission and verification that differ substantially from modern casualty accounting, and specialists in early Islamic history do not treat every individual figure in these sources as precise. The estimate is best understood as an order-of-magnitude claim, advanced primarily within Muslim scholarship as evidence of the restrained character of the era's warfare relative to the scale of territorial and political change it produced, rather than as an audited casualty count in the modern sense.

sections.title: The Herem Texts: Total Destruction Commanded
sections.body:
The Hebrew Bible's conquest narratives contain a small number of texts that, read at face value, describe the opposite of non-combatant protection: a divine command to destroy entire populations, explicitly including those who would elsewhere be classified as civilians. Deuteronomy 20:16-18 states: "But of the cities of these peoples, that Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance, you shall save alive nothing that breathes; but you shall utterly destroy them: the Hittite, the Amorite, the Canaanite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite; as Yahweh your God has commanded you; that they not teach you to follow all their abominations, which they have done to their gods; so would you sin against Yahweh your God." Here the herem, or "ban" — the practice of devoting a conquered population entirely to destruction — is commanded specifically against the named Canaanite peoples, without an exception for women, children, or any other otherwise-protected category; the stated rationale is the prevention of religious contamination, not military necessity.

1 Samuel 15:3 records an instance where a comparable command is applied outside Canaan proper. The prophet Samuel relays a divine instruction to King Saul concerning the Amalekites: "Now go and strike Amalek, and utterly destroy all that they have, and don’t spare them; but kill both man and woman, infant and nursing baby, ox and sheep, camel and donkey." This explicitly extends destruction to infants and nursing babies alongside livestock, and Saul's later partial non-compliance — sparing King Agag and the best of the animals — is presented in the subsequent narrative as the reason his kingship was rejected, meaning the text frames obedience to the command, not deviation from it, as the righteous course. The larger command to "remember what Amalek did" and to blot out the memory of Amalek, which Samuel's instruction to Saul carries out, is itself set out earlier in Deuteronomy 25:17-19: "Remember what Amalek did to you by the way as you came out of Egypt; how he met you by the way, and struck the rearmost of you, all who were feeble behind you, when you were faint and weary; and he didn’t fear God. Therefore it shall be, when Yahweh your God has given you rest from all your enemies all around, in the land which Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance to possess it, that you shall blot out the memory of Amalek from under the sky. You shall not forget."

A third, smaller-scale text is sometimes cited alongside the herem passages, though it is a different kind of text — an individual act of prophetic judgment rather than a war law or command to a national army. 2 Kings 2:23-24 states: "He went up from there to Bethel. As he was going up by the way, some youths came out of the city and mocked him, and said to him, 'Go up, you baldy! Go up, you baldy!' He looked behind him and saw them, and cursed them in Yahweh’s name. Then two female bears came out of the woods, and mauled forty-two of those youths." Unlike the Deuteronomy and 1 Samuel passages, this incident does not concern warfare or a divine command to an army; it is presented in the narrative as an act of prophetic retribution against mockery of the prophet Elisha, and it belongs to a broader set of ancient Near Eastern narrative conventions in which prophets pronounce curses that come to pass. It is nonetheless one of the texts most frequently raised in popular debate over violence against children in the Bible, and is included here at full strength alongside the war texts for that reason.

sections.title: Jewish and Christian Responses to the Herem Texts
sections.body:
Jewish and Christian interpreters have not treated the herem texts as settled or comfortable, and mainstream responses within both traditions engage them directly.

One influential line of Christian response, developed by the philosopher Paul Copan in Is God a Moral Monster? Making Sense of the Old Testament God, and further with the philosopher Matthew Flannagan in Did God Really Command Genocide? Coming to Terms with the Justice of God, argues that the herem commands employ standard ancient Near Eastern conquest rhetoric — a genre in which total-destruction language ("utterly destroy," "save alive nothing that breathes") functioned as a stock hyperbolic formula for decisive victory, comparable to similar total-annihilation language in other ancient Near Eastern royal inscriptions (such as the Moabite Mesha Stele) whose underlying events, on independent reconstruction, involved more limited routs and displacement than the literal wording suggests. On a related but distinct line of argument, the biblical scholar John Walton, with J. Harvey Walton, in The Lost World of the Israelite Conquest, argues that the Hebrew term herem itself means something closer to "removal from ordinary use" than "annihilate," pointing to internal biblical evidence — Joshua 11 describes northern cities as herem, yet only Hazor is actually burned; Leviticus 27:21 treats a herem field not as destroyed but as becoming priestly property — as evidence that the term functioned as a covenantal and theological category, marking the conquest as God's action rather than Israel's material gain, rather than as a literal mass-killing directive in every application.

This reading is contested within Christian and Jewish scholarship itself, not only by outside critics. The philosopher Randal Rauser, among others, has argued that the hyperbole and removal-from-use readings do not adequately answer the plain sense of commands directed at named categories, including infants and nursing children in 1 Samuel 15:3, and that treating the language as convention risks minimizing a textually explicit command rather than resolving the moral difficulty it raises. Jewish tradition, for its part, has generally treated the herem commands as bound to a specific, non-repeatable historical circumstance. Maimonides, reasoning from the biblical account that the Assyrian king Sennacherib mixed and displaced the ancient Near Eastern populations centuries later, ruled that the seven named Canaanite nations could no longer be reliably identified — rendering the command, in normative Jewish law, inapplicable in practice rather than a live instruction for any later era.

Within Christian theology specifically, a further line of response holds that the New Testament develops or reframes the ethic of the conquest texts. Matthew 5:44 records Jesus instructing his followers: "But I tell you, love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who mistreat you and persecute you." Mainstream Christian interpreters across a wide range of traditions read this instruction, part of the Sermon on the Mount, as establishing the governing ethic for Christian conduct — one that stands in visible tension with the herem texts. That tension is one Christian theologians have long debated rather than resolved by simple harmonization. The church's mainstream, across Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant traditions, has rejected the second-century teacher Marcion of Sinope's position that the Old Testament's God and law should be set aside altogether as belonging to a different, lesser deity than the New Testament reveals — a view condemned as heretical by the early church. Instead, the mainstream position has held both testaments as scripture while treating the New Testament's ethic of active enemy-love as, at minimum, the standard governing Christian conduct now, whatever conclusion one reaches about the original moral status of the conquest commands. This is a live and openly debated question within Christian scholarship, not a settled harmonization, and serious, faithful interpreters land in different places on it.

sections.title: Modern Invocations: Ancient Texts in Live Conflicts
sections.body:
Ancient religious war texts do not stay confined to ancient contexts. News organizations reported that Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, in a statement issued by his office on October 28, 2023, at the start of Israel's Gaza ground operation, told troops, in the government's own published English-language rendering, that they must "remember what Amalek has done" — a reference to the Deuteronomy and 1 Samuel texts discussed above — and that "we do remember and we are fighting." The remark, along with other statements by Israeli officials, was subsequently cited in South Africa's application to the International Court of Justice alleging incitement to genocide, an allegation Israel's government has denied, stating the reference was to Hamas rather than to Palestinians as a people. This article does not adjudicate that legal dispute; the point relevant here is narrower — that a specific ancient total-destruction text was invoked, on the public record, in a live twenty-first-century conflict, which is a different question from what the text was originally understood to require of ancient Israel.

On the human cost of a separate modern conflict, the Costs of War Project at the Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs, Brown University, estimates that direct violence in post-9/11 United States wars and counterterrorism operations in Iraq, Afghanistan, Syria, Yemen, and Pakistan — countries with majority-Muslim populations — killed more than 940,000 people between 2001 and 2023, of whom over 432,000 are estimated to have been civilians. The project separately estimates, using a multiplier methodology developed by the Geneva Declaration Secretariat (roughly four indirect deaths — from disease, displacement, and the destruction of health and economic infrastructure — for every direct death), that indirect deaths in these conflicts likely number 3.6 to 3.8 million, for a combined total the project describes as at least 4.5 to 4.7 million. The project's own researchers describe the direct-violence figure as a conservative undercount of the true toll and caution that the indirect-death estimate carries wide uncertainty inherent to the multiplier method used to produce it; these are the Costs of War Project's own stated figures and caveats, reported here with attribution rather than independently verified by this article.

A further modern comparison sometimes raised alongside religious war-ethics texts is the American atomic bombing of the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945 — an act of state warfare, not one framed by its planners in the religious terms discussed elsewhere in this article. Historians' estimates of the death toll vary considerably, given the destruction of civil records and the ongoing deaths from burns and radiation sickness through the end of 1945. The United States Manhattan Engineer District's contemporaneous 1946 tally put combined deaths at roughly 105,000 (66,000 at Hiroshima and 39,000 at Nagasaki), while later reassessments incorporating Japanese municipal records — including the Hiroshima Peace Memorial's estimate of roughly 140,000 dead by December 1945, and a later Japanese reassessment of roughly 70,000 dead at Nagasaki by the same date — put the combined total closer to 210,000. Historians commonly cite this full range, roughly 110,000 to 210,000, reflecting the gap between the earliest official tally and later, higher reconstructions. The large majority of the dead in both cities are estimated to have been civilians — in Hiroshima, an estimated 90 percent of deaths despite a military garrison of some 24,000 troops stationed in the city.

None of these modern episodes settles the textual and juristic questions addressed earlier in this article, and this article does not treat them as doing so. They are included because both religious traditions examined here have, at different points, been invoked by their own adherents, or by state and political actors operating within societies those traditions have shaped, in ways that sit uneasily beside the traditions' own texts of restraint. How a tradition's stated doctrine relates to the conduct of those who invoke its name, or its cultural inheritance, in a given era is a genuinely open question that recurs across both Islamic and Jewish/Christian history, and it is a different question from what the traditions' founding texts themselves say.

sections.title: Conclusion: Texts and Adherents, Judged Separately
sections.body:
Both traditions examined in this article contain strong resources for civilian protection, and both contain texts that sit uneasily beside that protection. Islamic law's textual and juristic case for non-combatant immunity is unusually explicit and well attested: Quranic verses restricting fighting to those who fight; a hadith on the prohibition of killing women and children carried by an unusually wide set of narrators; an early caliphal precedent, Abu Bakr's instructions to Yazid ibn Abi Sufyan's army, naming specific protected categories including monastics; and a genuine cross-school juristic consensus on the immunity of non-combatant women and children specifically. That case sits alongside the honest difficulty of Quran 9:5, which a real, named strand of classical exegesis read as abrogating much of this restraint — a reading disputed by the majority of scholars, ancient and modern, but not invented by modern extremists.

The Bible's herem texts present something like the reverse difficulty: read at face value, they describe exactly what Islamic law and modern international humanitarian law alike categorically forbid — the deliberate killing of women, children, and infants, framed as obedience to a divine command whose refusal costs a king his throne. Mainstream Jewish and Christian scholarship has not ignored this difficulty; it has produced substantial, contested interpretive work — the ancient-Near-Eastern-hyperbole reading, the removal-from-use reading of herem itself, the claim that Jewish law renders the command inapplicable in the absence of an identifiable Canaanite nation, and the claim that the New Testament's ethic of active enemy-love reframes or supersedes the conquest texts — none of which commands universal assent even within the traditions that produced it.

Neither tradition's founding texts, honestly read, hand their adherents an uncomplicated brief for or against civilian protection. Both offer real resources for restraint, and both contain material that must be interpreted, historically contextualized, or theologically reframed to arrive at a fully categorical protection doctrine. In the modern world, adherents of both traditions, and the political and military actors who invoke them, have — at various points and to greatly differing degrees — both honored and violated whatever protective doctrine their texts support: from extremist groups claiming Islamic warrant for attacks on civilians that classical jurists across every school would have called unlawful, to political rhetoric that reaches for ancient total-destruction language in a live war, to state violence, carried out by actors from across the religious spectrum, whose civilian death tolls in specific modern conflicts dwarf the casualty figures associated with the founding-era texts examined here. Whether a tradition should be judged chiefly by the most protective reading of its own founding texts, by its most permissive classical reading, or by the conduct of those who invoke its name in a given era is a question this article leaves open in both directions, for both traditions, because it is a fair question to put to each.

quranVerses.arabic: وَقَٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقَٰتِلُونَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ
quranVerses.translation: Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you but do not transgress. Indeed. Allah does not like transgressors.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: مِنْ أَجْلِ ذَٰلِكَ كَتَبْنَا عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنَّهُۥ مَن قَتَلَ نَفْسًۢا بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسَادٍۢ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّمَا قَتَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًۭا وَمَنْ أَحْيَاهَا فَكَأَنَّمَآ أَحْيَا ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًۭا ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا بِٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ ثُمَّ إِنَّ كَثِيرًۭا مِّنْهُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Because of that, We decreed upon the Children of Israel that whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land - it is as if he had slain mankind entirely. And whoever saves one - it is as if he had saved mankind entirely. And our messengers had certainly come to them with clear proofs. Then indeed many of them, [even] after that, throughout the land, were transgressors.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ عَٰهَدتُّم مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَنقُصُوكُمْ شَيْـًۭٔا وَلَمْ يُظَٰهِرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَدًۭا فَأَتِمُّوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلَىٰ مُدَّتِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ
quranVerses.translation: Excepted are those with whom you made a treaty among the polytheists and then they have not been deficient toward you in anything or supported anyone against you; so complete for them their treaty until their term [has ended]. Indeed, Allah loves the righteous [who fear Him].
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَإِذَا ٱنسَلَخَ ٱلْأَشْهُرُ ٱلْحُرُمُ فَٱقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ وَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱحْصُرُوهُمْ وَٱقْعُدُوا۟ لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍۢ ۚ فَإِن تَابُوا۟ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَخَلُّوا۟ سَبِيلَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌۭ رَّحِيمٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: And when the sacred months have passed, then kill the polytheists wherever you find them and capture them and besiege them and sit in wait for them at every place of ambush. But if they should repent, establish prayer, and give zakah, let them [go] on their way. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَإِنْ أَحَدٌۭ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ٱسْتَجَارَكَ فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّىٰ يَسْمَعَ كَلَٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ أَبْلِغْهُ مَأْمَنَهُۥ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌۭ لَّا يَعْلَمُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And if any one of the polytheists seeks your protection, then grant him protection so that he may hear the words of Allah. Then deliver him to his place of safety. That is because they are a people who do not know.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: But of the cities of these peoples, that Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance, you shall save alive nothing that breathes; but you shall utterly destroy them: the Hittite, the Amorite, the Canaanite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite; as Yahweh your God has commanded you; that they not teach you to follow all their abominations, which they have done to their gods; so would you sin against Yahweh your God.
Now go and strike Amalek, and utterly destroy all that they have, and don’t spare them; but kill both man and woman, infant and nursing baby, ox and sheep, camel and donkey.’”

He went up from there to Bethel. As he was going up by the way, some youths came out of the city and mocked him, and said to him, “Go up, you baldy! Go up, you baldy!” He looked behind him and saw them, and cursed them in Yahweh’s name. Then two female bears came out of the woods, and mauled forty-two of those youths.
But I tell you, love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who mistreat you and persecute you,

Remember what Amalek did to you by the way as you came out of Egypt; how he met you by the way, and struck the rearmost of you, all who were feeble behind you, when you were faint and weary; and he didn’t fear God. Therefore it shall be, when Yahweh your God has given you rest from all your enemies all around, in the land which Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance to possess it, that you shall blot out the memory of Amalek from under the sky. You shall not forget.
but the field, when it goes out in the Jubilee, shall be holy to Yahweh, as a devoted field. It shall be owned by the priests.

faq.question: Does Islamic law protect civilians even in verses like the "sword verse" (Quran 9:5)?
faq.answer: Most classical and nearly all contemporary Muslim scholars read 9:5 in context with the surrounding verses of the same passage, Quran 9:4 and 9:6, which respectively exempt treaty-honoring polytheists and grant protection to any individual seeking asylum. On this majority reading, 9:5 addresses specific Arabian tribes who had broken treaties and initiated hostilities, not non-combatants in general. A minority classical position, held by named exegetes including al-Dahhak ibn Muzahim and reported by al-Tabari, read 9:5 as abrogating much earlier restraint; this was a genuine strand of classical legal theory but not the majority view, then or now.

faq.question: What hadith establish the prohibition on killing women and children in war?
faq.answer: The core report is attributed to Ibn Umar, who said that a woman was found killed during one of the Prophet's expeditions, after which the Prophet denounced the killing and forbade the killing of women and children in war. This is recorded in both Sahih al-Bukhari (hadith 3014) and Sahih Muslim (hadith 1744), with additional narrations on the same point attributed to several other companions.

faq.question: Is there full agreement among Islamic jurists on which non-combatants are protected?
faq.answer: There is a genuine cross-school consensus (ijma), recorded by the jurist Ibn Rushd in Bidayat al-Mujtahid, that non-combatant women and children may not be killed. The schools disagreed, however, on the legal rationale for the rule, which affected less clear-cut categories such as elderly non-combatants, monastics, and hired laborers; most later fiqh manuals extended protection to these groups as well, but the strict point of unanimous consensus is narrower than the full list.

faq.question: How do Christians and Jews explain the Bible's herem ("total destruction") commands?
faq.answer: Responses vary and remain debated. Some scholars, such as Paul Copan and Matthew Flannagan, argue the language is ancient Near Eastern hyperbole describing more limited routs. John Walton argues herem means "removal from use" rather than literal annihilation in every case. Critics such as Randal Rauser argue these readings do not fully answer the text's plain sense. Jewish tradition, following Maimonides, holds the command inapplicable since the ancient Canaanite nations can no longer be identified. Many Christians additionally hold that the New Testament's ethic of loving one's enemies (Matthew 5:44) governs Christian conduct regardless of how the conquest texts are read.

faq.question: How many people were killed across all of Prophet Muhammad's military campaigns?
faq.answer: Scholars working from classical Muslim historical sources (the maghazi and sira literature) have produced estimates on the order of one thousand total deaths on all sides combined, across some eighty expeditions over more than two decades — for example, Muhammad Hamidullah's count of about 150 Muslim dead, or Maulana Wahiduddin Khan's combined total of 259 Muslims and 759 non-Muslims (1,018 total). These figures should be treated as order-of-magnitude estimates drawn from sources compiled one to two centuries after the events, not as an audited modern casualty count.

faq.question: Have these ancient religious war texts been invoked in modern conflicts?
faq.answer: Yes. News coverage reported that Israel's Prime Minister invoked the biblical Amalek narrative in an October 2023 statement at the start of the Gaza war, a reference later cited in South Africa's genocide case before the International Court of Justice, which Israel disputes. Separately, named research projects and historians have documented very large civilian death tolls in other modern conflicts, including post-9/11 wars (per the Costs of War Project at Brown University) and the 1945 atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki. None of this settles what the ancient texts themselves originally required; it shows that the interpretive questions this article discusses are not merely academic.

furtherReading.title: Battlefields of the Prophet Muhammad: A Contribution to Muslim Military History
furtherReading.author: Muhammad Hamidullah
furtherReading.note: Historical study computing troop numbers and casualties across the Prophet's military campaigns from classical Arabic sources.

furtherReading.title: Did God Really Command Genocide? Coming to Terms with the Justice of God
furtherReading.author: Paul Copan and Matthew Flannagan
furtherReading.note: Book-length philosophical and theological defense of reading the herem texts as bounded ancient Near Eastern conquest rhetoric (Baker Books, 2014).

furtherReading.title: The Lost World of the Israelite Conquest: Covenant, Retribution, and the Fate of the Canaanites
furtherReading.author: John H. Walton and J. Harvey Walton
furtherReading.note: Argues that herem means removal from ordinary use rather than literal annihilation in every instance, using internal biblical evidence (IVP Academic, 2017).

furtherReading.title: Arguing the Just War in Islam
furtherReading.author: John Kelsay
furtherReading.note: Scholarly study of the shari'a-based jurisprudential tradition on war and its rules protecting non-combatants (Harvard University Press, 2007).
